segments.title: Designing a Royal Navy within a whole-defence budget
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke frames his fleet as a practical force-structure exercise: capabilities must be affordable, mutually supporting, and argued for without attacking the other services.
segments.body: The proposed navy begins with a constraint often lost in force-design debates: public money must cover the whole of national defence and other public needs. Every proposed capability must therefore be justifiable, based on existing or projected equipment, and proportionate rather than an unconstrained fantasy fleet. He uses the CVA-01 dispute to argue that inter-service competition can leave every service weaker, since the Royal Navy lost its carriers while the Royal Air Force failed to secure the strike aircraft and soon lost much of its long-range bomber capability. The larger lesson is that a naval case should explain what the fleet contributes to national defence rather than dismissing aircraft, armour, education, health, policing, or other legitimate claims on taxation.

evidence.note: Introduces the force-design criteria and the argument against inter-service funding battles.

segments.title: A five-ship core for global reach
segments.summary: The proposed fleet is built around two strike carriers and three large amphibious ships, with logistics and escorts treated as the means that make distant power useful.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares a carrier force to an American-football team whose quarterback cannot function without protection from the offensive line. The five-ship core comprises two Queen Elizabeth-class strike carriers and three large amphibious ships, creating the mobile reach around which the rest of the fleet is organized. Royal Fleet Auxiliary support and escorts are not secondary extras, because fuel, stores, protection, and troop movement determine whether the headline ships can influence events far from Britain. The watch point is useful for understanding force structure as a mutually supporting system rather than a shopping list of prestigious hulls.

evidence.note: Sets out the proposed force categories and the supporting role of logistics and escorts.

segments.title: Queen Elizabeth-class air groups as mixed aviation forces
segments.summary: A carrier air group is presented as a balance of F-35B fighters, anti-submarine Merlins, commando-lift helicopters, and airborne early-warning aircraft rather than a fighter count alone.
segments.body: The discussion treats the Queen Elizabeth class as having space for a substantial mixed air group rather than a fixed fighter-only load. Dr. Clarke sketches two F-35B squadrons alongside Merlin helicopters configured for anti-submarine warfare, commando lift, and airborne early warning. Those roles connect fleet air defence and strike with submarine detection, troop movement, and surveillance beyond the horizon. The example shows why carrier capacity must be judged by the combination of missions it can sustain, not simply by its fast-jet count.

evidence.note: Describes the proposed Queen Elizabeth-class troop capacity and mixed F-35B and Merlin air group.

segments.title: Canberra-class ships as amphibious and aviation insurance
segments.summary: Three Canberra-class-sized landing helicopter docks would combine dock, stores, accommodation, hangar, and flight deck capacity while providing limited back-up for a carrier or amphibious ship in refit.
segments.body: The proposed LHD force is intended to move a self-contained commando-centred formation with aircraft, landing capability, stores, accommodation, and logistics in one hull. A Canberra-sized ship and a Bay-class successor could carry roughly a third to half of a modern brigade, including artillery and manoeuvre elements. Dr. Clarke stresses reciprocal resilience: an LHD can temporarily provide limited F-35B strike-carrier capability, while a Queen Elizabeth-class carrier can partly cover aviation and troop roles when enough dock landing ships are present. He also distinguishes the LHD as a flexible response to many non-peer threats from the strike carrier needed when modern missiles and other high-end weapons make opposition more capable.

evidence.note: Explains Canberra-class LHD capacity, task-force scale, and limited substitution with the carriers.

segments.title: Forward-based amphibious shipping and fleet sustainment
segments.summary: Forward-based landing and logistics ships would handle routine regional tasks, leaving carrier and amphibious task groups available as a larger reaction force.
segments.body: The proposal places smaller, persistent capabilities in areas such as the Indian Ocean, South Atlantic, and Pacific so that a major task group is not required for every incident. Forward-based landing and logistics ships could handle a limited emergency, support an ally, or provide a regional foothold while the carriers and LHD task groups remain reaction forces. Dr. Clarke then argues for eight Tide-class tankers and four solid-support ships to sustain operations at global range. Pacific distance and the possibility of wartime losses make replenishment capacity a resilience requirement rather than a peacetime efficiency calculation.

evidence.note: Covers forward-based landing ships, tanker numbers, and additional solid-support ships.

segments.title: Three escort groups for carrier and amphibious task forces
segments.summary: The escort proposal uses six air-defence destroyers and nine Type 26 frigates to form three high-end groups, with Type 45 successors and Type 31s adding flexibility and presence.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke’s arithmetic starts with the escorts needed to protect carriers and LHDs, not with isolated ship totals. Each of three high-end groups would combine two Type 45 air-defence destroyers with three Type 26 anti-submarine frigates. That structure could support a carrier battle group and an amphibious task group simultaneously while retaining another formation for availability, allied operations, or regional commitments. Canadian, Australian, American, and other allied escorts could slot into the same formation when a larger screen was needed. Type 31s could reinforce these groups as close escorts in war, while future Type 45 successors could also provide an occasional high-end regional presence.

evidence.note: Sets out the proposed Type 45, Type 26, and Type 31 numbers and their task-group roles.

segments.title: Personnel growth as the cost of a larger fleet
segments.summary: The larger escort and task-group structure would require roughly 25 percent more Royal Navy personnel, making recruiting and crewing part of the capability proposal rather than an invisible assumption.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pauses the hull arithmetic to acknowledge that ships cannot be added without crews. His rough estimate is that the proposed fleet would require about a 25 percent increase in Royal Navy personnel. Recruiting, training, and retaining that larger workforce would therefore be a substantial programme in its own right. The moment is a useful corrective to force plans that count platforms while leaving their human operating requirement unstated.

evidence.note: Estimates the personnel increase required by the proposed fleet and connects it to recruiting and operational options.

segments.title: Type 31s for persistent regional presence
segments.summary: A larger Type 31 force would keep ships in the Falklands, Gibraltar, Bahrain, and Singapore, giving Britain a visible regional stake before a crisis demands a task group.
segments.body: The watch point distinguishes routine presence from high-end warfighting: forward-based Type 31s can observe, train, reassure partners, and provide an initial response. Pairs at the Falklands, Gibraltar, Bahrain, and Singapore would cover the South Atlantic and Antarctic approaches, Mediterranean trade, the Gulf and Indian Ocean, and Southeast Asia. The remaining ships would refit and train crews in Britain while providing a home-water escort when another power sends a major warship near the coast. Dr. Clarke’s political point is that alliance membership does not substitute for being physically present if Britain wants an informed and credible voice in regional decisions.

evidence.note: Explains forward basing and presence missions for ten to twelve Type 31 frigates.

segments.title: A modular River-class patrol force
segments.summary: A modified Batch 3 River class could combine heavier guns, Martlet missiles, StanFlex modules, UAV support, a stern ramp, and optional towed sensors for flexible regional patrol work.
segments.body: The concept is deliberately based on existing equipment rather than a wholly new design, using modules to adapt a patrol ship to the local mission. Dr. Clarke proposes a twin 40-millimetre Bofors mounting, two 30-millimetre guns with Martlet missiles, three StanFlex positions, a UAV hangar, and a stern ramp. The ramp could deploy boats, unmanned systems, or a towed sonar according to the task. This smaller-vessel layer could escort mine-countermeasures forces, counter piracy, protect merchant shipping, exercise with partners, and visit ports too small for a Type 31.

evidence.note: Describes the modified River-class equipment fit and its flexible patrol roles.

segments.title: Naval stations as diplomatic and humanitarian networks
segments.summary: Regional naval stations led by senior captains would coordinate ships, diplomats, partner relationships, disaster relief, and the advance knowledge that makes a fleet more useful in a crisis.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke revives the station idea for a force spread across Bahrain, Singapore, the Falklands, Gibraltar, and home waters. A senior captain holding a commodore's pennant would coordinate each region without requiring a permanent one-star appointment. Joint staffs would work closely with the Foreign Office, using port visits, exercises, shipboard meetings, and naval hospitality to open diplomatic access. Disaster-relief modules, cranes, boats, landing ships, and Royal Marines would add a humanitarian role, while persistent local relationships could provide warning and access before a larger crisis develops.

evidence.note: Outlines regional stations, command appointments, diplomacy, and disaster-relief roles.

segments.title: Amphibious vehicles for Royal Marines and strike brigades
segments.summary: Using amphibious combat vehicles across Royal Marine and strike formations would let troops drive ashore, reinforce one another, and reduce dependence on landing craft for every movement.
segments.body: The proposal favours the US Marine Corps Amphibious Combat Vehicle for a force comprising an amphibious brigade and two strike-amphibious brigades. Common vehicles would let Royal Marines seize an entry point and allow Army formations arriving on Point-class ships to reinforce them with compatible equipment. Heavy assets could still use landing craft, but personnel carriers could move directly from ship to shore or cross waterways without waiting for every lift serial. Dr. Clarke invokes the loss of Sir Galahad to emphasize the danger of troops remaining concentrated aboard a transport while awaiting disembarkation.

evidence.note: Makes the case for common amphibious vehicles across Royal Marine and strike formations.

segments.title: Continuous deterrence and convertible submarine tubes
segments.summary: The deterrent argument centres on keeping a ballistic-missile submarine continuously at sea, while using some compatible boats as cruise-missile platforms and reserves against accidents or refit delays.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts an always-deployed deterrent with weapons that must be activated or sent out during a crisis, which he sees as a more escalatory signal. Four boats are normally associated with guaranteeing continuous at-sea deterrence, but his proposed fleet would build five compatible hulls while publicly assigning only three to the ballistic-missile role. The other two would carry cruise-missile inserts in their large tubes, adding useful conventional first-day strike capacity. They could revert to the deterrent mission if collision, refit delay, or another accident made one of the three SSBNs unavailable.

evidence.note: Discusses continuous at-sea deterrence, SSBN availability, and cruise-missile conversion.

segments.title: Britain's nuclear-submarine shipyard limits fleet numbers
segments.summary: Britain's single nuclear-submarine construction yard constrains how quickly SSBNs, Astute-class boats, and a future attack-submarine class can be built, even when a larger fleet is desirable.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke says he would like more nuclear attack submarines but treats industrial capacity as a binding constraint. Britain has one yard producing nuclear submarines, so completing seven Astute-class boats while building five Dreadnought-type hulls would already impose a heavy workload. A successor attack-submarine class of seven to nine boats would have to follow within that same production system. The point connects force ambition to the continuity, throughput, and skilled workforce of the submarine shipyard rather than assuming orders can instantly become operational boats.

evidence.note: Explains how the single nuclear-submarine construction yard constrains SSBN, Astute, and successor production.

segments.title: Conventional-submarine partnership and Falklands basing
segments.summary: For a conventional submarine successor, Dr. Clarke considers Japanese designs or a joint sensor-led project, paired with forward basing in the Falklands to strengthen South Atlantic awareness.
segments.body: The discussion sees a partner programme as a way to combine British sensor expertise with an available Japanese conventional-submarine design. Britain could buy an established Soryu-derived option or join Japan's proposed 3,000-ton successor programme and integrate an Astute-style sensor suite. Dr. Clarke suggests that Canada, and perhaps Australia if its contemporary programme continued to struggle, might find a joint design attractive. Forward-basing two boats in the Falklands would add South Atlantic awareness and deterrence while four remained available from Britain.

evidence.note: Covers Japanese conventional-submarine options and the rationale for Falklands forward basing.

segments.title: National presence within allied collective strength
segments.summary: Alliances multiply British power, but dependence on American logistics and post-Cold War European cuts mean Britain still needs its own regional forces if it wants influence and warning.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames Falklands basing as an attempt to build strength and relationships before the South Atlantic or Antarctica becomes a sharper arena of competition. He argues that collective Western power rests heavily on American logistics, bases, forces, and money. European governments could legitimately reduce defence spending after the Cold War, but in his view many cut global presence too far. In a globally connected society, alliance membership does not remove the need for national ships and submarines if Britain wants local knowledge, diplomatic weight, and a say in how a crisis is managed.

evidence.note: Connects South Atlantic basing to Chinese competition, American alliance support, European defence cuts, and the need for national presence.

segments.title: Royal Marine battle groups and strategic lift
segments.summary: Four regular and two reserve Royal Marine battle groups could form larger amphibious formations, but their scale depends on Point-class strategic lift as well as LHD and dock-landing-ship availability.
segments.body: The proposal organizes four regular and two reserve Royal Marine commandos as self-contained battle groups, three of which could combine into a brigade. In normal availability conditions, two LHDs and two Bay-class successors would carry only part of that maximum formation. Point-class ships would provide the additional strategic lift needed to deploy the remaining troops and equipment. Dr. Clarke therefore calls for six future lift ships with better accommodation, making transport capacity and living conditions part of the force-structure calculation rather than an afterthought.

evidence.note: Summarizes the Royal Marine battle-group structure and dependence on Point-class lift.

segments.title: A joint P-8 force for maritime patrol
segments.summary: The P-8 proposal doubles the regular fleet and adds a reserve component, with aircraft based for reach from Diego Garcia, the Falklands, and the United Kingdom.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke proposes sixteen regular P-8 Poseidons and eight reserve aircraft, tripling the planned airframe total while doubling the regular establishment. Basing or detachments at Diego Garcia, the Falklands, and in Britain would extend maritime patrol coverage and provide surge capacity. Similarities between the P-8 and civilian Boeing airliners are used to support a larger reservist contribution. Because the aircraft performs an essential maritime mission, he argues that Royal Navy and Royal Air Force personnel should jointly crew and advocate for the force.

evidence.note: Sets out a larger P-8 fleet, regional basing, reserves, and a joint-service operating model.

segments.title: Mine countermeasures and survey ships for port access
segments.summary: Mine countermeasures vessels, hydrographic survey ships, and small training craft are treated as the unglamorous capabilities that keep ports, sea lanes, and deployments usable.
segments.body: The plan keeps four mine-countermeasures vessels in the Gulf and adds enough capacity for a Singapore detachment and British home waters. Dr. Clarke names Portsmouth, Plymouth, Southampton, and the Medway as places where mines could quickly obstruct naval or commercial movement. He also retains the hydrographic survey squadron, including the large ocean-survey ship and smaller Echo-class vessels, because charting and seabed knowledge support access and operations. Twelve Archer-type craft would maintain university and reserve training links that a larger combatant fleet still needs.

evidence.note: Discusses P-8 support roles, mine countermeasures, survey ships, and Archer-class training vessels.

segments.title: A global Royal Marine regiment for smaller missions
segments.summary: Small Royal Marine companies embarked on forward-deployed landing ships would give the fleet a rotating force for independent regional missions while preserving larger commandos for battle groups.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes a regiment of company-sized units from the commandos that form the core of amphibious battle groups. Each forward-deployed landing or support ship would normally carry a company commanded by a major for smaller independent missions. Eight companies would sustain four deployed units while allowing relief, training, and recovery at home. The separate four regular and two reserve commandos would remain available to form the larger amphibious battle groups and brigade structure.

evidence.note: Explains the proposed global regiment, company deployments, rotation, and commando structure.
